Name: dynamic_kv_cache_layout
Title: Dynamic KV-Cache Layout Optimization for Efficient Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adjustment of KV-cache layouts based on real-time profiler data can significantly improve transformer inference efficiency by optimizing memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy.
Related Work: Existing works like FlashInfer and BitDecoding optimize KV-cache storage and leverage tensor cores, but they rely on static configurations or low-bit quantization. Our proposal introduces a dynamic approach that adjusts layouts in real-time based on profiler metrics such as warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity. This distinguishes our work by enabling adaptive optimization tailored to specific hardware conditions and workloads.
Abstract: Transformer inference efficiency is critically dependent on the effective utilization of GPU resources, particularly memory bandwidth and tensor cores. Current approaches often use static KV-cache layouts or low-bit quantization, which may not adapt to varying workloads or hardware conditions. We propose a dynamic KV-cache layout optimization framework that leverages real-time profiler data to adjust layouts on-the-fly. Our framework monitors metrics such as warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity, and dynamically reconfigures the KV-cache layout to minimize HBM round-trips and maximize tensor-core utilization. We implement this approach using CUDA/Triton kernels and evaluate it on a commodity GPU across multiple transformer models and workloads. Our experiments demonstrate significant improvements in wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second, with detailed profiler traces attributing gains to specific hardware optimizations. This work bridges model compression with deployment systems engineering, providing a hardware-faithful solution for efficient transformer inference.
Experiments: Baseline Comparison: Measure wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second for static KV-cache layouts (e.g., FlashInfer) and low-bit quantization (e.g., BitDecoding) on LLaMA-2 and GPT-3 models.

Dynamic Layout Optimization: Implement and evaluate our dynamic layout framework, comparing it to baselines under fixed accuracy budgets. Use nvprof/Nsight traces to measure occupancy, HBM bandwidth, and tensor-core utilization.
Workload Variability: Test the framework under varying sequence lengths and batch sizes to assess its adaptability.
Hardware-Specific Evaluation: Evaluate the framework on multiple GPU architectures (e.g., Ampere, Hopper) to validate its generalizability.
Profiler Metrics: Collect detailed metrics such as warp stall reasons, arithmetic intensity, and coalesced loads to attribute performance gains to specific hardware optimizations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Overhead of Real-Time Profiling: Continuous monitoring may introduce overhead, potentially offsetting performance gains.
Hardware Dependence: The framework's effectiveness may vary across GPU architectures, limiting its generalizability.
Complexity of Implementation: Dynamic layout adjustment requires sophisticated kernel-level optimizations, increasing implementation complexity.
Workload Sensitivity: The framework's performance may degrade under certain workloads or edge cases, necessitating further tuning.
